texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: THE SCHR ¨ ODINGER BRIDGE PROBLEM FOR JUMP DIFFUSIONS WITH REGIME SWITCHING
texts.text: THE SCHR ¨ ODINGER BRIDGE PROBLEM FOR JUMP DIFFUSIONS WITH REGIME SWITCHING

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ANDREI ZLOTCHEVSKI AND LINAN CHEN
texts.text: ANDREI ZLOTCHEVSKI AND LINAN CHEN

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract. The Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (SBP) aims at finding the measure ̂ P on a certain path space which possesses the desired state-space distributions ρ 0 at time 0 and ρ T at time T while minimizing the KL divergence from a reference path measure R . This work focuses on the SBP in the case when R is the path measure of a jump diffusion with regime switching, which is a Markov process that combines the dynamics of a jump diffusion with interspersed discrete events representing changing environmental states. To the best of our knowledge, the SBP in such a setting has not been previously studied.
texts.text: Abstract. The Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (SBP) aims at finding the measure ̂ P on a certain path space which possesses the desired state-space distributions ρ 0 at time 0 and ρ T at time T while minimizing the KL divergence from a reference path measure R . This work focuses on the SBP in the case when R is the path measure of a jump diffusion with regime switching, which is a Markov process that combines the dynamics of a jump diffusion with interspersed discrete events representing changing environmental states. To the best of our knowledge, the SBP in such a setting has not been previously studied.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of the dynamics of the SBP solution ̂ P in the regime-switching jump-diffusion setting. In particular, we show that ̂ P is again a path measure of a regime-switching jump diffusion; under proper assumptions, we establish various properties of ̂ P from both a stochastic calculus perspective and an analytic viewpoint. In addition, as a demonstration of the general theory developed in this work, we examine a concrete unbalanced SBP (uSBP) from the angle of a regimeswitching SBP, where we also obtain novel results in the realm of uSBP.
texts.text: In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of the dynamics of the SBP solution ̂ P in the regime-switching jump-diffusion setting. In particular, we show that ̂ P is again a path measure of a regime-switching jump diffusion; under proper assumptions, we establish various properties of ̂ P from both a stochastic calculus perspective and an analytic viewpoint. In addition, as a demonstration of the general theory developed in this work, we examine a concrete unbalanced SBP (uSBP) from the angle of a regimeswitching SBP, where we also obtain novel results in the realm of uSBP.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our topic of interest is the Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (SBP) for regime-switching jump diffusions. As an extension of the framework of the jump-diffusion SBP [46], the formulation adopted in this work incorporates additional layers of complexity in order to model realworld phenomena present under changing environmental states. To begin, jump diffusions themselves generalize classical diffusions by integrating jumps driven by L´ evy measures, thereby capturing sudden events such as market crashes or neuron firings. Regime switching further enhances this by having the drift, diffusion, and jump coefficients vary according to a Markov chain on a finite set of states called regimes , which represents sudden environmental changes. We are interested in studying the SBP in such a setting. Even though the general study of the SBP has a long and rich history, the regime-switching SBP has not seen much development in the literature. This work is dedicated to introducing a general theory on the SBP for regime-switching jump diffusions.
texts.text: Our topic of interest is the Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (SBP) for regime-switching jump diffusions. As an extension of the framework of the jump-diffusion SBP [46], the formulation adopted in this work incorporates additional layers of complexity in order to model realworld phenomena present under changing environmental states. To begin, jump diffusions themselves generalize classical diffusions by integrating jumps driven by L´ evy measures, thereby capturing sudden events such as market crashes or neuron firings. Regime switching further enhances this by having the drift, diffusion, and jump coefficients vary according to a Markov chain on a finite set of states called regimes , which represents sudden environmental changes. We are interested in studying the SBP in such a setting. Even though the general study of the SBP has a long and rich history, the regime-switching SBP has not seen much development in the literature. This work is dedicated to introducing a general theory on the SBP for regime-switching jump diffusions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1.1. Set-up of the problem. Our model is set on a 'hybrid' state space R d ×S over a finite time interval t ∈ [0 , T ], where S is a finite set consisting of all the regimes. Without loss of generality, we will assume that S = { 1 , 2 , . . . , |S|} . Let Ω = D ([0 , T ]; R d ×S ) be the Skorokhod space, i.e., the space of c` adl` ag functions ω : t ∈ [0 , T ] ↦→ ( ω R d ( t ) , ω S ( t )) ∈ R d ×S , equipped with the Skorokhod metric. For every t ∈ [0 , T ], define ( X t , Λ t ) : Ω → R d × S to be the time projection such that, for every ω ∈ Ω, X t ( ω ) = ω R d ( t ) and Λ t ( ω ) = ω S ( t ). If F := B Ω is the Borel σ -algebra on Ω, then F is generated by the time projections { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] . Let {F t } [0 ,T ] be the natural filtration (or its usual augmentation) associated with { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] . Denote by P (Ω) the set of probability measures on (Ω , F ). For P ∈ P (Ω), to which we refer as a path measure , we say that { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] ( ω ) is the canonical process under P if ω ∈ Ω is sampled under P . Finally, for 0 ≤ t ≤ T , we write the distribution of ( X t , Λ t ) under P as P t , and call it the marginal distribution at time t .
texts.text: 1.1. Set-up of the problem. Our model is set on a 'hybrid' state space R d ×S over a finite time interval t ∈ [0 , T ], where S is a finite set consisting of all the regimes. Without loss of generality, we will assume that S = { 1 , 2 , . . . , |S|} . Let Ω = D ([0 , T ]; R d ×S ) be the Skorokhod space, i.e., the space of c` adl` ag functions ω : t ∈ [0 , T ] ↦→ ( ω R d ( t ) , ω S ( t )) ∈ R d ×S , equipped with the Skorokhod metric. For every t ∈ [0 , T ], define ( X t , Λ t ) : Ω → R d × S to be the time projection such that, for every ω ∈ Ω, X t ( ω ) = ω R d ( t ) and Λ t ( ω ) = ω S ( t ). If F := B Ω is the Borel σ -algebra on Ω, then F is generated by the time projections { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] . Let {F t } [0 ,T ] be the natural filtration (or its usual augmentation) associated with { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] . Denote by P (Ω) the set of probability measures on (Ω , F ). For P ∈ P (Ω), to which we refer as a path measure , we say that { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] ( ω ) is the canonical process under P if ω ∈ Ω is sampled under P . Finally, for 0 ≤ t ≤ T , we write the distribution of ( X t , Λ t ) under P as P t , and call it the marginal distribution at time t .

texts.text: Definition 1.1.1. Given a path measure R ∈ P (Ω) and two probability distributions ρ 0 , ρ T on R d ×S , the Schr¨ odinger bridge problem is to find the path measure ̂ P ∈ P (Ω) such that

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the above SBP, we refer to R as the reference measure , ρ 0 , ρ T as the target distributions , and the solution ̂ P , if it exists, as the Schr¨ odinger bridge . Heuristically speaking, the SBP is the quest to find the path 'bridging' ρ 0 and ρ T that is the 'closest' to a given reference.
texts.text: In the above SBP, we refer to R as the reference measure , ρ 0 , ρ T as the target distributions , and the solution ̂ P , if it exists, as the Schr¨ odinger bridge . Heuristically speaking, the SBP is the quest to find the path 'bridging' ρ 0 and ρ T that is the 'closest' to a given reference.

texts.orig: In this paper, we consider the SBP with the reference measure R arising from a regimeswitching jump diffusion , which is a two-component strong Markov † process { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] (defined on some generic probability space) with ( R d ×S )-valued c` adl` ag sample paths. Below we will explain each component of this process.
texts.text: In this paper, we consider the SBP with the reference measure R arising from a regimeswitching jump diffusion , which is a two-component strong Markov † process { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] (defined on some generic probability space) with ( R d ×S )-valued c` adl` ag sample paths. Below we will explain each component of this process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the SBP studied in this work, we consider a reference measure R ∈ P (Ω) whose canonical process { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] is a regime-switching jump diffusion in the sense described above, i.e., as a process on (Ω , F , {F t } [0 ,T ] , R ), { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] is a strong Markov process satisfying the SDE (1.1.4). In other words, R is a strong Markov path measure. For every ( t, x, i ) ∈ [0 , T ] × R d ×S , we denote by R t,x,i the conditional distribution of R conditioning on ( X t , Λ t ) = ( x, i ). We refer to R 0 and R T as the initial data and the terminal data respectively, and write R 0 T for the joint distribution of R at the endpoints t = 0 and t = T .
texts.text: For the SBP studied in this work, we consider a reference measure R ∈ P (Ω) whose canonical process { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] is a regime-switching jump diffusion in the sense described above, i.e., as a process on (Ω , F , {F t } [0 ,T ] , R ), { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] is a strong Markov process satisfying the SDE (1.1.4). In other words, R is a strong Markov path measure. For every ( t, x, i ) ∈ [0 , T ] × R d ×S , we denote by R t,x,i the conditional distribution of R conditioning on ( X t , Λ t ) = ( x, i ). We refer to R 0 and R T as the initial data and the terminal data respectively, and write R 0 T for the joint distribution of R at the endpoints t = 0 and t = T .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1.2. Static Aspect of the SBP. In the previous part we introduced the SBP in the regimeswitching jump-diffusion setting. In order to conduct a proper study of this problem, we require some basic conditions to guarantee that both the SBP (1.1.1) and the SDE (1.1.4) are 'solvable' with solutions in desired forms. To this end, we adopt the following general assumptions for the rest of the work.
texts.text: 1.2. Static Aspect of the SBP. In the previous part we introduced the SBP in the regimeswitching jump-diffusion setting. In order to conduct a proper study of this problem, we require some basic conditions to guarantee that both the SBP (1.1.1) and the SDE (1.1.4) are 'solvable' with solutions in desired forms. To this end, we adopt the following general assumptions for the rest of the work.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Assumption (G) .
texts.text: Assumption (G) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ( G1 ) For every ( t, x, i ) ∈ [0 , T ] × R d ×S , the SDE (1.1.4) admits a solution † P t,x,i ∈ P (Ω) where the canonical process { ( X r , Λ r ) } [0 ,T ] under P t,x,i satisfies (1.1.4) for r ∈ [ t, T ] with ( X t , Λ t ) ≡ ( x, i ). Moreover, the family { P t,x,i } [0 ,T ] × R d ×S is strong Markovian in the sense that for every stopping time τ ∈ [ t, T ], the conditional distribution of P t,x,i conditioning on F τ is given by P τ,X τ , Λ τ .
texts.text: ( G1 ) For every ( t, x, i ) ∈ [0 , T ] × R d ×S , the SDE (1.1.4) admits a solution † P t,x,i ∈ P (Ω) where the canonical process { ( X r , Λ r ) } [0 ,T ] under P t,x,i satisfies (1.1.4) for r ∈ [ t, T ] with ( X t , Λ t ) ≡ ( x, i ). Moreover, the family { P t,x,i } [0 ,T ] × R d ×S is strong Markovian in the sense that for every stopping time τ ∈ [ t, T ], the conditional distribution of P t,x,i conditioning on F τ is given by P τ,X τ , Λ τ .

texts.orig: 1.3. Main Results. Below we give an overview of the main results obtained in this work. First, we examine various properties of the Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P in the general regimeswitching jump-diffusion setting. Then, we put the general theory into practice by applying it to a concrete example in the context of the unbalanced SBP . Our results generally extend the existing literature on the diffusion (and jump-diffusion) SBP to the regime-switch setting. Our unbalanced SBP example is also a novel model in its own realm.

Our results generally extend the existing literature on the diffusion (and jump-diffusion) SBP to the regime-switch setting. Our unbalanced SBP example is also a novel model in its own realm.

texts.orig: where R , g , and { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] are the same as in the previous subsection. By invoking Assumption ( H ) (the harmonic assumption), which endows φ with desirable properties, we establish the SDE formulation of ̂ P . In particular, we discover in Theorem 2.2.1 that the canonical process under ̂ P is again a regime-switching jump diffusion, satisfying an SDE in the same form as (1.1.4) but with new coefficients in terms of φ , from where we also obtain the explicit expression of the infinitesimal generator associated with ̂ P . Our main methodology is to adapt stochastic calculus tools for jump diffusions to the regimeswitching model. In fact, we establish that ̂ P can be viewed as a Girsanov transform from (a modification of) the reference measure R , where the 'change of measure' can be made explicit in terms of φ , and it affects not only the drift and the jump component of the original SDE (1.1.4), but also its regime-switching mechanism.

texts.orig: Stochastic control formulation of SBP. In Section 2.3, we take the aforementioned Girsanov transform approach further and develop a stochastic control formulation of our SBP. Upon strengthening Assumption ( H ), we prove in Theorem 2.3.3 that the specific Girsanov transform which yields ̂ P can in fact be attained by solving a properly crafted Stochastic Control Problem . This confirms that the rich connection between the SBP and the field of stochastic control extends to the regime-switching setting.

This confirms that the rich connection between the SBP and the field of stochastic control extends to the regime-switching setting.

texts.orig: The Fortet-Sinkhorn algorithm. Also under the presence of the transition density function, in Section 3.3 we formulate the Fortet-Sinkhorn algorithm in the regime-switch setting. This celebrated algorithm enables one to solve the Schr¨ odinger system iteratively in a computationally efficient manner [4] to obtain ( φ, ̂ φ ) and thus to determine the Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P . In Theorem 3.3.1 , we prove that the multi-regime version of the algorithm converges to a unique solution under conditions analogous to those in the single-regime case.
texts.text: The Fortet-Sinkhorn algorithm. Also under the presence of the transition density function, in Section 3.3 we formulate the Fortet-Sinkhorn algorithm in the regime-switch setting. This celebrated algorithm enables one to solve the Schr¨ odinger system iteratively in a computationally efficient manner [4] to obtain ( φ, ̂ φ ) and thus to determine the Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P . In Theorem 3.3.1 , we prove that the multi-regime version of the algorithm converges to a unique solution under conditions analogous to those in the single-regime case.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The unbalanced SBP. In Section 4, we apply the general theory from the previous sections to study an unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (uSBP) originated from a jump diffusion with killing. It is natural to fit such a process into our regime-switching model with two regimes only - 'active' and 'dead', where the regime-switching mechanism is reduced to a simple form governed by the killing rate. We set up the uSBP in a novel way that, at the terminal time, not only the position of 'active' particles but also the killing position of 'dead' particles are required to match the target distribution. By adapting the existing regularity theory of jump diffusions to the case with killing, we can configure the uSBP model so that all the assumptions invoked in the general theory become accessible, in the sense that they are either directly fulfilled or verifiable by the target distribution. Moreover, most of the general results from the previous sections, including the stochastic control formulation, the transition (and marginal) density function of ̂ P , the dynamic Schr¨ odinger system of ( φ, ̂ φ ), the Schr¨ odinger bridge forward PIDE, etc., will take explicit forms in the uSBP setting. Some possible variations of this uSBP will also be discussed.
texts.text: The unbalanced SBP. In Section 4, we apply the general theory from the previous sections to study an unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (uSBP) originated from a jump diffusion with killing. It is natural to fit such a process into our regime-switching model with two regimes only - 'active' and 'dead', where the regime-switching mechanism is reduced to a simple form governed by the killing rate. We set up the uSBP in a novel way that, at the terminal time, not only the position of 'active' particles but also the killing position of 'dead' particles are required to match the target distribution. By adapting the existing regularity theory of jump diffusions to the case with killing, we can configure the uSBP model so that all the assumptions invoked in the general theory become accessible, in the sense that they are either directly fulfilled or verifiable by the target distribution. Moreover, most of the general results from the previous sections, including the stochastic control formulation, the transition (and marginal) density function of ̂ P , the dynamic Schr¨ odinger system of ( φ, ̂ φ ), the Schr¨ odinger bridge forward PIDE, etc., will take explicit forms in the uSBP setting. Some possible variations of this uSBP will also be discussed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 1.4. Motivation, Background and Assumptions. Regime-switching (jump) diffusions form a rich class of stochastic processes that capture a broad range of applications in operations management [38], biology [27, 40], financial modeling [35, 44], actuarial science [17], etc. In contrast to single-regime models, regime-switching jump diffusions provide a framework for modeling discrete events that affect the 'environment' in which the stochastic process is evolving. As a result, multi-regime models enable a more realistic representation of the dynamical system. In the last thirty years, regime-switching diffusions and jump diffusions have seen increasing interest, especially for optimization and control problems. In fact, the SBP belongs to both of these categories: it is an optimization problem in its primal formulation and a control problem in its stochastic control formulation. The SBP is thus highly relevant for both modeling and control purposes in regime-switching applications.
texts.text: 1.4. Motivation, Background and Assumptions. Regime-switching (jump) diffusions form a rich class of stochastic processes that capture a broad range of applications in operations management [38], biology [27, 40], financial modeling [35, 44], actuarial science [17], etc. In contrast to single-regime models, regime-switching jump diffusions provide a framework for modeling discrete events that affect the 'environment' in which the stochastic process is evolving. As a result, multi-regime models enable a more realistic representation of the dynamical system. In the last thirty years, regime-switching diffusions and jump diffusions have seen increasing interest, especially for optimization and control problems. In fact, the SBP belongs to both of these categories: it is an optimization problem in its primal formulation and a control problem in its stochastic control formulation. The SBP is thus highly relevant for both modeling and control purposes in regime-switching applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The SBP has a rich history going back to E. Schr¨ odinger's original thought experiment in the 1930s [36, 37] and has generated a large body of research (e.g. [2, 4, 10, 15, 19, 33]), especially in the diffusion case. As regime-switching models become more and more widely adopted, we believe it is necessary to expand existing knowledge on the SBP in this direction. On the other hand, introducing regime switching to a (jump) diffusion adds yet another layer of complexity that imposes new technical challenges. The purpose of this work is twofold: first, we aim to establish fundamental theoretical results for the SBP for regimeswitching (jump) diffusions under assumptions that are analogous to the single-regime SBP. Second, we show how an unbalanced SBP can be modeled via a regime-switching approach, and we provide a detailed analysis that establishes new results beyond the existing literature on this topic.
texts.text: The SBP has a rich history going back to E. As regime-switching models become more and more widely adopted, we believe it is necessary to expand existing knowledge on the SBP in this direction. On the other hand, introducing regime switching to a (jump) diffusion adds yet another layer of complexity that imposes new technical challenges. The purpose of this work is twofold: first, we aim to establish fundamental theoretical results for the SBP for regimeswitching (jump) diffusions under assumptions that are analogous to the single-regime SBP. Second, we show how an unbalanced SBP can be modeled via a regime-switching approach, and we provide a detailed analysis that establishes new results beyond the existing literature on this topic.

texts.orig: Despite the general literature being scarce compared to that of diffusions, the field of jump diffusions, with and without regime switching, is fast developing and the research body is expanding in both the depth and the diversity. With this in mind, instead of restricting ourselves to a rigid set-up, we put the basic requirements only (Assumption (C) ) on the model under consideration so that our work can potentially reach a broad class of regimeswitching jump diffusions. On the other hand, when exploring certain aspects of the SBP, in order to establish the desired results, we will invoke assumptions in terms of the specific properties required of our model, and these properties may be fulfilled in several ways, as we will briefly explain below.
texts.text: Despite the general literature being scarce compared to that of diffusions, the field of jump diffusions, with and without regime switching, is fast developing and the research body is expanding in both the depth and the diversity. With this in mind, instead of restricting ourselves to a rigid set-up, we put the basic requirements only (Assumption (C) ) on the model under consideration so that our work can potentially reach a broad class of regimeswitching jump diffusions. On the other hand, when exploring certain aspects of the SBP, in order to establish the desired results, we will invoke assumptions in terms of the specific properties required of our model, and these properties may be fulfilled in several ways, as we will briefly explain below.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To start, Assumption ( G1 ) calls for the existence and uniqueness of the solution to the SDE (1.1.4). The solution theory for general jump diffusions is well studied; in particular, it is known that (1.1.4) admits a unique ( strong ) solution under Lipschitz-type conditions on the coefficients (see, e.g., [1, Chapter 6.2]). Specifically in the regime-switching setting, results on the existence and uniqueness of solutions can be found in [3, 22, 23, 42, 43, 45], among others. In fact, for results in Section 2.1 and discussions in Section 3, ( G1 ) can be relaxed to the wellposedness of the martingale problem associated with the SDE (1.1.4) † , which is known to hold when the coefficients are bounded and continuous in both time and space (e.g., [20, 39]).
texts.text: To start, Assumption ( G1 ) calls for the existence and uniqueness of the solution to the SDE (1.1.4). The solution theory for general jump diffusions is well studied; in particular, it is known that (1.1.4) admits a unique ( strong ) solution under Lipschitz-type conditions on the coefficients (see, e.g., [1, Chapter 6.2]). Specifically in the regime-switching setting, results on the existence and uniqueness of solutions can be found in [3, 22, 23, 42, 43, 45], among others. In fact, for results in Section 2.1 and discussions in Section 3, ( G1 ) can be relaxed to the wellposedness of the martingale problem associated with the SDE (1.1.4) † , which is known to hold when the coefficients are bounded and continuous in both time and space (e.g., [20, 39]).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As for Assumption ( G2 ), we rely on it, together with the Markov property of the SDE solution, to obtain the Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P in the desired static form (1.2.1), which serves as the starting point of our investigation. However, this is not the only way to achieve (1.2.1); ̂ P is known to hold such a representation under different combinations of conditions. For a detailed discussion, we refer readers to [28, Section 2].
texts.text: As for Assumption ( G2 ), we rely on it, together with the Markov property of the SDE solution, to obtain the Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P in the desired static form (1.2.1), which serves as the starting point of our investigation. However, this is not the only way to achieve (1.2.1); ̂ P is known to hold such a representation under different combinations of conditions. For a detailed discussion, we refer readers to [28, Section 2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section 2, in order to develop a stochastic analysis approach to the SBP, we will impose Assumption ( H ) (and its strengthening ( H' )), which requires the function φ defined in (1.3.1) to possess sufficient regularity (in the temporal and the spatial variables) and to be harmonic in the sense that φ satisfies the Kolmogorov backward equation corresponding to the SDE (1.1.4). This is the case for a wide range of diffusions [10, 19]. As for jump diffusions, while the regularity theory is still developing, there have been many results on the Kolmogorov backward equation in recent years ([6-8, 46]).
texts.text: In Section 2, in order to develop a stochastic analysis approach to the SBP, we will impose Assumption ( H ) (and its strengthening ( H' )), which requires the function φ defined in (1.3.1) to possess sufficient regularity (in the temporal and the spatial variables) and to be harmonic in the sense that φ satisfies the Kolmogorov backward equation corresponding to the SDE (1.1.4). This is the case for a wide range of diffusions [10, 19]. As for jump diffusions, while the regularity theory is still developing, there have been many results on the Kolmogorov backward equation in recent years ([6-8, 46]).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A common scenario in which the regularity and the harmonic property of φ can be achieved is when the reference measure R admits a transition density function that is sufficiently regular and the function g is bounded; this is exactly the motivation of Assumption (D) and its strengthening ( ̂ D )), which are the main assumptions in Section 3. In the current literature, there are results on the existence and the regularity of the transition density function for single-regime jump diffusions (e.g. [8, 21, 43]). In the case of time-homogeneous jump diffusions, the method in [41, Section 5] offers a way to derive the multi-regime transition density function in the case when every 'fixed regime' jump diffusion (i.e., the SDE (1.1.4) without the regime-switching component and with the remaining coefficients restricted to a fixed regime) admits a transition density function. In addition, when the regime-switching mechanism is simple (e.g., the uSBP model considered in Section 4), it is also possible to extend the results on the transition density function of a single-regime jump diffusion to the multi-regime setting.

In the current literature, there are results on the existence and the regularity of the transition density function for single-regime jump diffusions (e.g. [8, 21, 43]). In the case of time-homogeneous jump diffusions, the method in [41, Section 5] offers a way to derive the multi-regime transition density function in the case when every 'fixed regime' jump diffusion (i.e., the SDE (1.1.4) without the regime-switching component and with the remaining coefficients restricted to a fixed regime) admits a transition density function. In addition, when the regime-switching mechanism is simple (e.g., the uSBP model considered in Section 4), it is also possible to extend the results on the transition density function of a single-regime jump diffusion to the multi-regime setting.

texts.orig: 1.5. Notation and Terminology. For a function ( t, x, i ) ∈ [0 , T ] × R d ×S ↦→ f ( t, x, i ) ∈ R , we denote by ∇ f, ∇ 2 f the gradient and the Hessian respectively of f with respect to the spatial variable x only.
texts.text: 1.5. Notation and Terminology. For a function ( t, x, i ) ∈ [0 , T ] × R d ×S ↦→ f ( t, x, i ) ∈ R , we denote by ∇ f, ∇ 2 f the gradient and the Hessian respectively of f with respect to the spatial variable x only.

texts.orig: We say that a function f on [0 , T ] × R d ×S is of class C 1 , 2 if, for every i ∈ S , f ( · , i ) ∈ C ([0 , T ] × R d ), and as a function on (0 , T ) × R d , f ( · , i ) is once differentiable in the temporal variable and twice differentiable in the spatial variable with ∂ ∂t f ( · , i ) , ∇ f ( · , i ) , ∇ 2 f ( · , i ) ∈ C ((0 , T ) × R d ). If, in addition, f ( · , i ) is supported on a compact subset of (0 , T ) × R d , then we say that f is of class C 1 , 2 c .
texts.text: We say that a function f on [0 , T ] × R d ×S is of class C 1 , 2 if, for every i ∈ S , f ( · , i ) ∈ C ([0 , T ] × R d ), and as a function on (0 , T ) × R d , f ( · , i ) is once differentiable in the temporal variable and twice differentiable in the spatial variable with ∂ ∂t f ( · , i ) , ∇ f ( · , i ) , ∇ 2 f ( · , i ) ∈ C ((0 , T ) × R d ). If, in addition, f ( · , i ) is supported on a compact subset of (0 , T ) × R d , then we say that f is of class C 1 , 2 c .

texts.orig: As stated in Section 1.3, throughout this work, Assumptions (C) and (G) are in effect for the SDE (1.1.4) and the SBP (1.1.1). Given a path measure R whose canonical process { ( X t , Λ t ) } [0 ,T ] is a strong Markov process satisfying (1.1.4), and two target distributions ρ 0 , ρ T , recall from Section 1.2 that the unique solution to (1.1.1), also called the Schr¨ odinger bridge, is given by ̂ P = f ( X 0 , Λ 0 ) g ( X T , Λ T ) R , where f , g are a solution to the static Schr¨ odinger system (1.2.2). We will first carry out some preliminary analysis on ̂ P based on this static viewpoint.

texts.orig: 2.2. Schr¨ odinger Bridge SDE and Generator. To study the Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P as in (2.1.2), we first approach it from an SDE viewpoint. More specifically, we will interpret ̂ P as a Girsanov transform from P 0 , based on which we will derive the SDE that governs the canonical process under ̂ P . However, to achieve this goal, we need to impose additional conditions on the function φ defined in (1.3.1) and the generator L as in (2.1.6), so that we can fit the formulation (2.1.2) of ̂ P into the Girsanov transform framework. To this end, the following assumption will be invoked in this subsection.
texts.text: 2.2. Schr¨ odinger Bridge SDE and Generator. To study the Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P as in (2.1.2), we first approach it from an SDE viewpoint. More specifically, we will interpret ̂ P as a Girsanov transform from P 0 , based on which we will derive the SDE that governs the canonical process under ̂ P . However, to achieve this goal, we need to impose additional conditions on the function φ defined in (1.3.1) and the generator L as in (2.1.6), so that we can fit the formulation (2.1.2) of ̂ P into the Girsanov transform framework. To this end, the following assumption will be invoked in this subsection.

However, in view of the Schr¨ odinger system (1.2.2), only the restriction of g ( · , i ) on supp( R T ( · , i )) is concerned in solving the SBP, and the value of g ( · , i ) elsewhere has no impact. Therefore, the C 1 , 2 -requirement in ( H ) at t = T should be understood as lim t ↗ T φ ( t, · , i ) = g ( · , i ) R T -a.e.

On the other hand, the positivity of φ required in Assumption ( H ) can be fulfilled, for example, when the target distributions ρ 0 , ρ T are such that g > 0 everywhere, or the reference measure R has an everywhere positive transition distribution.

texts.orig: Proof. This result is established by [46, Theorem 4.2] for jump diffusions without regime switching. We extend the proof to cover the regime-switching case. In particular, we will show that under Assumption ( H ), when restricted to F T -, ̂ P is in fact a Girsanov transform from P 0 with ( u, θ, ξ ) explicitly determined by φ . Throughout the proof, whenever possible, we suppress the argument of γ ( t, x, i, z ), β ( t, x, i, w ), and α ( t, x, i, w ) to simplify the notation.

texts.orig: Remark. In the above discussion of the Girsanov transform perspective of the Schr¨ odinger bridge, we have excluded the endpoints t = 0 and t = T (since we already know that the endpoint distributions are ρ 0 , ρ T ), and focused only on the dynamics of the 'bridge'. However, if φ is sufficiently regular such that Itˆ o's formula can be applied to {-log( φ ( t, X t , Λ t )) } [0 ,T ] (with t = 0 , t = T included), then (2.2.9) holds for all 0 ≤ t < s ≤ T and hence, with ( u, θ, ξ ) as in (2.2.10), Z ( u,θ,ξ ) 0 ,T = φ ( T,X T , Λ T ) φ (0 ,X 0 , Λ 0 ) and E P 0 [ Z ( u,θ,ξ ) 0 ,T ] = 1. In this case, the Girsanov transform formulation of ̂ P also extends to the endpoints in the sense that, ̂ P = Z ( u,θ,ξ ) 0 ,T P 0 is a Girsanov transform from P 0 as a path measure on D ([0 , T ]; R d ×S ). This will be the set-up of our work in the next subsection.
texts.text: Remark. In the above discussion of the Girsanov transform perspective of the Schr¨ odinger bridge, we have excluded the endpoints t = 0 and t = T (since we already know that the endpoint distributions are ρ 0 , ρ T ), and focused only on the dynamics of the 'bridge'. However, if φ is sufficiently regular such that Itˆ o's formula can be applied to {-log( φ ( t, X t , Λ t )) } [0 ,T ] (with t = 0 , t = T included), then (2.2.9) holds for all 0 ≤ t < s ≤ T and hence, with ( u, θ, ξ ) as in (2.2.10), Z ( u,θ,ξ ) 0 ,T = φ ( T,X T , Λ T ) φ (0 ,X 0 , Λ 0 ) and E P 0 [ Z ( u,θ,ξ ) 0 ,T ] = 1. In this case, the Girsanov transform formulation of ̂ P also extends to the endpoints in the sense that, ̂ P = Z ( u,θ,ξ ) 0 ,T P 0 is a Girsanov transform from P 0 as a path measure on D ([0 , T ]; R d ×S ). This will be the set-up of our work in the next subsection.

texts.orig: Proof. Clearly, under Assumption ( H' ), the triple ( u ∗ , θ ∗ , ξ ∗ ) given by (2.3.6) satisfies the first two conditions in the definition of U . Moreover, by the remark below Assumption ( H' ), the third condition of U also holds for ( u ∗ , θ ∗ , ξ ∗ ) due to the relation (2.2.9) with t = 0 , s = T . Therefore, we have that ( u ∗ , θ ∗ , ξ ∗ ) ∈ U . Since (2.3.6) coincides with (2.2.10), the last statement of Theorem 2.3.3 follows immediately from Theorem 2.2.1. In addition, we also have that ( u ∗ , θ ∗ , ξ ∗ ) ∈ U ( ρ T ).
texts.text: Proof. Clearly, under Assumption ( H' ), the triple ( u ∗ , θ ∗ , ξ ∗ ) given by (2.3.6) satisfies the first two conditions in the definition of U . Moreover, by the remark below Assumption ( H' ), the third condition of U also holds for ( u ∗ , θ ∗ , ξ ∗ ) due to the relation (2.2.9) with t = 0 , s = T . Therefore, we have that ( u ∗ , θ ∗ , ξ ∗ ) ∈ U . Since (2.3.6) coincides with (2.2.10), the last statement of Theorem 2.3.3 follows immediately from Theorem 2.2.1. In addition, we also have that ( u ∗ , θ ∗ , ξ ∗ ) ∈ U ( ρ T ).

texts.orig: In this section, we will consider the situation when the solution to the SDE (1.1.4) admits a transition density function, in which case the Schr¨ odinger bridge of the corresponding SBP (1.1.1) is more tractable, and additional information on its dynamics becomes accessible.
texts.text: In this section, we will consider the situation when the solution to the SDE (1.1.4) admits a transition density function, in which case the Schr¨ odinger bridge of the corresponding SBP (1.1.1) is more tractable, and additional information on its dynamics becomes accessible.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3.2. The Backward and Forward PIDEs. Continuing under the setting of the previous subsection, the goal of this part is to establish the partial integro-differential equations (PIDEs) that the Schr¨ odinger potentials ( φ, ̂ φ ) satisfy. These PIDEs should be viewed as the continuum-time extension of the ( φ, ̂ φ )-Schr¨ odinger system (3.1.3), and they characterize the dynamics, both forward and backward in time, of the regime-switching jump diffusion process under the Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P . In order to establish these PIDEs, we will work with a set of strengthened assumptions compared to those in the previous subsection.
texts.text: 3.2. The Backward and Forward PIDEs. Continuing under the setting of the previous subsection, the goal of this part is to establish the partial integro-differential equations (PIDEs) that the Schr¨ odinger potentials ( φ, ̂ φ ) satisfy. These PIDEs should be viewed as the continuum-time extension of the ( φ, ̂ φ )-Schr¨ odinger system (3.1.3), and they characterize the dynamics, both forward and backward in time, of the regime-switching jump diffusion process under the Schr¨ odinger bridge ̂ P . In order to establish these PIDEs, we will work with a set of strengthened assumptions compared to those in the previous subsection.

We want to determine the counterpart for the 'forward' dynamics. We expect that the forward generator is given by the adjoint of L , but we first need to identify a proper notion of adjoint operator in the regime-switching setting.

texts.orig: Remark. Assumption ( ̂ D ), which consists of ( ̂ D1 ) and ( D2 ), provides a set of sufficient conditions for the regularity of ( φ, ̂ φ ) required in later discussions. As we will see in the next section, we can readily find regime-switching jump diffusion models that satisfy Assumption ( ̂ D ). However, these conditions are not unique. Some variations of ( ̂ D1 ) and ( D2 ) may also support the main theorem below. For example, if p ij in ( ̂ D1 ) satisfies the heat kernel estimate, then ( D2 ) may be relaxed to f , g having at most polynomial growth at infinity; or, if the function ( t, x, i ) ↦→ p ij ( t, x, T, · ) is harmonic, then the C 1 , 2 b -requirement in ( ̂ D1 )(a) on the function ( t, x ) ↦→ p ij ( t, x, T, · ) may be replaced by a C 1 , 2 -requirement.

texts.orig: The existence and uniqueness of a solution to (3.1.3), as well as how to find the solution, is a long-studied problem in the history of the SBP [2, 4, 15, 18]. One approach is to construct recursively a sequence of functions that converges to a solution of (3.1.3). This is the idea of the Fortet-Sinkhorn algorithm , originally proposed in [15], which consists of alternating between the four equations (3.1.3). Under certain conditions, the algorithm that consists of repeatedly applying the map C will in fact converge to the unique fixed point ̂ φ ( T, · ), with any starting function. This algorithm is well-understood and studied in many SBP contexts [4, 5, 15], as well as beyond † . Below, we formulate this convergence result rigorously in the regime-switching setting.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. An Example: The Unbalanced Schr¨ odinger Bridge Problem
texts.text: 4. An Example: The Unbalanced Schr¨ odinger Bridge Problem

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section we present an application of the regime-switching jump-diffusion SBP investigated in the previous sections in the context of the unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (uSBP). Unlike classical SBPs aiming at 'bridging' probability distributions, the model of uSBP arises from the study of stochastic particle systems with mass loss , which results in the terminal distribution being a sub-probability measure (i.e., with total mass less than 1). Such a mechanism can be naturally interpreted in terms of regime switching, where we view the mass loss as the consequence of particles switching from the active regime to the dead regime. Based on this consideration, we will adopt the theory of regime-switching SBP developed above to examine a uSBP corresponding to a jump diffusion with killing.
texts.text: In this section we present an application of the regime-switching jump-diffusion SBP investigated in the previous sections in the context of the unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge problem (uSBP). Unlike classical SBPs aiming at 'bridging' probability distributions, the model of uSBP arises from the study of stochastic particle systems with mass loss , which results in the terminal distribution being a sub-probability measure (i.e., with total mass less than 1). Such a mechanism can be naturally interpreted in terms of regime switching, where we view the mass loss as the consequence of particles switching from the active regime to the dead regime. Based on this consideration, we will adopt the theory of regime-switching SBP developed above to examine a uSBP corresponding to a jump diffusion with killing.

On the other hand, because ψ 12 ( t, x ) = x for every ( t, x ) ∈ [0 , T ] × R d and there is no dynamic in the dead regime, while R T ( · , 1) corresponds to the terminal position of the active particles, R T ( · , 2) is exactly the distribution of the 'killing' location of the dead particles. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Background on uSBP. Stochastic dynamical systems with absorbing states or disappearing particles are ubiquitous in nature. These situations can range from radioactive decay [47] to cellular event triggers [16] to oceanic current tracers sinking [5]. On the other hand, jump diffusion models with killing provide a mathematically rich framework for modeling such dynamical systems where the stochastic motion is described by a combination of drift, diffusion, and/or jump mechanics. The uSBP for this class of stochastic processes thus becomes an important tool for constructing models that are consistent with observational data. A 'modern' version of the uSBP was introduced in [5] as a mathematical model following Schr¨ odinger's original quest ‡ , where the reference measure R is given by a diffusion process in R d with killing, and the target distributions ρ 0 , ρ T prescribe the distribution of the surviving particles and the total amount of mass lost by time T . This problem was approached by augmenting R d to include a 'coffin state' c to which the process would jump upon being killed, and then treated as a classic SBP for c` adl` ag paths in R d ∪ { c } . This version of the uSBP was then further studied in different contexts including neural networks [32], entropic optimal transport [25], and random walks on directed graphs [11]. A variation of the uSBP was examined in [12] and [13] where the target at the terminal time is imposed only on the killing time and the killing location of the dead particles (but not the distribution of the surviving particles).

On the other hand, jump diffusion models with killing provide a mathematically rich framework for modeling such dynamical systems where the stochastic motion is described by a combination of drift, diffusion, and/or jump mechanics. The uSBP for this class of stochastic processes thus becomes an important tool for constructing models that are consistent with observational data. A 'modern' version of the uSBP was introduced in [5] as a mathematical model following Schr¨ odinger's original quest ‡ , where the reference measure R is given by a diffusion process in R d with killing, and the target distributions ρ 0 , ρ T prescribe the distribution of the surviving particles and the total amount of mass lost by time T . This problem was approached by augmenting R d to include a 'coffin state' c to which the process would jump upon being killed, and then treated as a classic SBP for c` adl` ag paths in R d ∪ { c } . This version of the uSBP was then further studied in different contexts including neural networks [32], entropic optimal transport [25], and random walks on directed graphs [11]. A variation of the uSBP was examined in [12] and [13] where the target at the terminal time is imposed only on the killing time and the killing location of the dead particles (but not the distribution of the surviving particles).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Compared with the version of uSBP in [5], we add two additional layers of complexity to our uSBP model (4.0.3): first, the reference measure R is a jump-diffusion with killing; second, the terminal target distribution ρ T not only encodes the distribution of the surviving particles, but also records where the dead particles were killed. In a forthcoming paper, we will take one step further to study uSBPs under a diversity of settings. This will help shed light on the connections among existing uSBP works.

In a forthcoming paper, we will take one step further to study uSBPs under a diversity of settings. This will help shed light on the connections among existing uSBP works.

texts.orig: Remark. The existence of the transition density function q ( t, x, s, y ) under killing can be extended to the case when the killing rate is spatial-dependent, i. e., V = V ( t, x ) as a continuous and bounded function in ( t, x ) ∈ [0 , T ] × R d . Indeed, q ( t, x, s, y ) can be constructed from q 0 ( t, x, s, y ) following the standard Duhamel's method, but the regularity theory of q ( t, x, s, y ) is more involved in such a case. For simplicity, we only consider the spatialhomogeneous killing rate in our uSBP example. However, if q 0 ( t, x, s, y ) is known to satisfy some refined estimates (e.g., certain heat kernel estimates), then the results below also extend to the case with a spatial-dependent killing rate.
texts.text: Remark. Indeed, upon extending L 0 from regime '1' to regime '2' trivially, i.e., setting all the coefficients of L 0 to zero in regime '2', and taking into account the 'one-way' (from regime '1' to regime '2') only switching mechanism, we see that the above uSBP Schr¨ odinger system is exactly (3.2.7) where the jump diffusion component of L is simply L 0 , and the regime-switching rates are Q 12 = V, Q 21 = 0.
texts.text: Remark. Although seemingly different, the above system is consistent with the Schr¨ odinger system (3.2.7) stated in the general theory. Indeed, upon extending L 0 from regime '1' to regime '2' trivially, i.e., setting all the coefficients of L 0 to zero in regime '2', and taking into account the 'one-way' (from regime '1' to regime '2') only switching mechanism, we see that the above uSBP Schr¨ odinger system is exactly (3.2.7) where the jump diffusion component of L is simply L 0 , and the regime-switching rates are Q 12 = V, Q 21 = 0.

texts.orig: 4.4. Further Questions on the uSBP. In this example, we discussed one specific uSBP scenario: when the target is on the spatial distributions of the surviving and the killed particles. Since the target distributions are often extracted from the actual observations of the system, a further question that is worth exploring is how to adapt the uSBP formulation to scenarios where the observations provide access to different levels of information about the particles. As mentioned earlier, a few examples involving different observational constraints have been addressed in the literature [5, 12, 13], but a general investigation of this question is still missing. We hope to establish a unified framework, based on the regime-switching approach, to systematically study and compare uSBPs in a broad range of target distribution scenarios. The key observation is that by varying ψ ( t, x ), we can choose which information to 'record' about the killed particles, and this exact information will be exhibited in the terminal distribution in regime 2. In this way, we can treat events that occur in the interior of the path as part of the reference terminal data, and 'match' it with our target when solving the uSBP.
texts.text: 4.4. Further Questions on the uSBP. In this example, we discussed one specific uSBP scenario: when the target is on the spatial distributions of the surviving and the killed particles. Since the target distributions are often extracted from the actual observations of the system, a further question that is worth exploring is how to adapt the uSBP formulation to scenarios where the observations provide access to different levels of information about the particles. As mentioned earlier, a few examples involving different observational constraints have been addressed in the literature [5, 12, 13], but a general investigation of this question is still missing. We hope to establish a unified framework, based on the regime-switching approach, to systematically study and compare uSBPs in a broad range of target distribution scenarios. The key observation is that by varying ψ ( t, x ), we can choose which information to 'record' about the killed particles, and this exact information will be exhibited in the terminal distribution in regime 2. In this way, we can treat events that occur in the interior of the path as part of the reference terminal data, and 'match' it with our target when solving the uSBP.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The regime-switching approach also enables us to study uSBP models in which, in addition to active particles being killed, the dead particles are also allowed to revive. This is done simply by changing the regime-switching rate Q 21 to be non-zero. The general theory of the regime-switching SBP can then be applied to derive properties of the unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge in this setting. Moreover, it is also possible to distinguish between 'revived' and 'never-killed' particles by introducing a third regime and letting Q 23 be the revival rate (instead of Q 21 ).
texts.text: The regime-switching approach also enables us to study uSBP models in which, in addition to active particles being killed, the dead particles are also allowed to revive. This is done simply by changing the regime-switching rate Q 21 to be non-zero. The general theory of the regime-switching SBP can then be applied to derive properties of the unbalanced Schr¨ odinger bridge in this setting. Moreover, it is also possible to distinguish between 'revived' and 'never-killed' particles by introducing a third regime and letting Q 23 be the revival rate (instead of Q 21 ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In summary, the regime-switching approach may lead to a deeper and more comprehensive theory of the uSBP, and we hope this work helps build its foundations.
